turns.content: I started using Anki but I'm drowning in reviews. Am I making cards wrong?

Usually, yes—either the cards are too hard/ambiguous, you’re adding too many per day, or your settings are causing too many early repetitions. “Drowning” is almost always a workload math problem plus card design. To diagnose quickly: 1) How many new cards per day are you adding (or how many notes are you importing)? 2) Roughly how many reviews are you doing daily, and how long does it take? 3) Are you using mostly cloze deletions or Q→A cards? 4) When you fail a card, why—did you never learn it, is it poorly worded, or is it multiple facts at once?

I’m adding like 40 new a day because I feel behind. Reviews are 300-600 depending on the day and it can take 2-3 hours. Mostly cloze. When I fail it’s usually like… I kind of know it but I can’t recall the exact wording or there are too many blanks.

That pattern is classic: high new-card intake + cloze overload + “exact wording” friction. You can fix this without quitting. Here’s what’s happening: - 40 new/day compounds fast. With typical retention targets, each new card generates multiple reviews in the next week. If your average card causes, say, 7–10 reviews in its first month, 40/day becomes 280–400 additional monthly reviews per day once it stabilizes. That’s before older backlog. - Clozes often encourage “fill-in-the-blank perfection,” which punishes near-misses and creates false difficulty. - “Too many blanks” violates minimum information principle: one card should test one idea. Steps I’d take this week: 1) Immediately drop new cards to 5–10/day (or even 0 for 3 days) to stop the bleeding. 2) Change how you grade: if you know the concept but miss a minor wording detail, that’s often “Hard,” not “Again,” provided your card is actually testing understanding. 3) Fix card design: split multi-blank clozes into smaller units; avoid clozes that require verbatim recall. 4) Triage: suspend low-value cards and leeches. If you tell me your current settings (learning steps, graduating interval, easy interval, max interval, lapse steps, leech threshold), I can recommend a sane baseline.
I haven’t touched settings. I’m on whatever defaults are. I don’t even know what half those mean. Where do I find them?

In Anki (desktop), click the gear icon next to your deck → Options. Defaults vary by version, but you’ll see sections like “New Cards,” “Reviews,” “Lapses,” and sometimes “Advanced.” Quick glossary: - Learning steps: short intervals (minutes/days) a new card goes through before it “graduates” to regular scheduling. - Graduating interval: first interval after you complete learning steps. - Easy interval: first interval if you hit “Easy” on a new card. - Lapse steps: what happens when you fail a mature card (Again). - Leech threshold: after N lapses, Anki tags/suspends the card. A “sane baseline” for overwhelmed learners is usually: - New cards/day: 5–15 - Learning steps: something like 10m, 1d (or 10m, 1d, 3d if you want more) - Graduating interval: 3d - Easy interval: 4–7d - Lapse steps: 10m (maybe 1d too), and a new interval around 70% - Max reviews/day: either unlimited or high, but manage by controlling new cards instead But settings are only half. Card quality is the bigger lever. Can you describe what one of your “too many blanks” cards looks like?
Like I’ll have a sentence with maybe 4 clozes in it. Sometimes it’s a list of steps and every step is blanked. Or I take a paragraph and blank out key terms. I thought that was efficient.

It feels efficient because you create fewer cards, but it’s inefficient for your brain because you’re forcing a big recall set in one go. That creates: - high failure rate, - low confidence, - lots of “Again,” - and huge review load. Better pattern: one cloze per card (or at most two if they’re tightly linked and always recalled together). If you have a list of steps, make each step its own card, or use “enumeration” cards carefully. Concrete redesign options: 1) One-cloze-per-sentence: - Bad: “The process has steps: {{c1::A}}, {{c2::B}}, {{c3::C}}, {{c4::D}}.” - Better: four cards: “Step 1 is {{c1::A}}.” etc. 2) “Cue → target” rather than paragraph cloze: - Front: “What’s the purpose of X?” - Back: “To do Y (in order to Z).” This tests meaning, not verbatim placement. 3) For lists: use “set completion” sparingly. - Front: “Name the 4 steps of the process.” - Back: “A, B, C, D.” But then accept partial recall: if you get 3/4, mark Hard and immediately do a follow-up card that isolates the missing one. Otherwise you’ll keep failing it. If you want, describe a specific list-type card and I can show you how I’d split it.
It’s like “Factors that influence [thing] include: A, B, C, D, E” and I blank all of them. Then when I review I can remember maybe 3 but not all 5 and I hit Again and it comes back constantly.

Yeah—those are review traps. A five-item all-or-nothing card is basically designed to generate lapses. Better approaches: Option A: Break into atomic cards (recommended) Make five cards: - “One factor that influences [thing] is {{c1::A}}.” - “One factor that influences [thing] is {{c1::B}}.” … etc. To avoid losing the “set” structure, add a separate card: - Front: “How many main factors influence [thing]?” - Back: “Five: A–E.” (This helps you keep the mental grouping without making recall all-or-nothing.) Option B: Use incremental list recall Create a series that builds: - Card 1: “Name 2 factors…” Back: “A, B” - Card 2: “Name 3 factors…” Back: “A, B, C” … up to 5. This reduces early failure but increases total cards. Only do this if the list is truly foundational. Option C: Use “one missing item” cloze Instead of blanking all five, show four and ask for the missing one: - “Factors include A, B, C, D, {{c1::E}}.” Then create multiple versions where each item is missing. This is good because the cue is strong and you’re practicing discrimination. Most overwhelmed users should start with Option A or C. Option C often feels easiest and reduces lapses. Also: stop grading 3/5 as “Again.” If the card is designed as all-or-nothing, it’s punishing you. Redesign it so your grading matches reality.
I feel like if I split it into five cards I’ll explode my deck size and then I’ll have even more reviews.

Deck size can grow while reviews shrink if the cards become easier and you stop failing them. The review count is driven more by difficulty and lapses than raw number of cards. Think of it like this: - One “5-in-1” card that you fail often will reappear a lot (and can eat minutes per encounter because you’re reconstructing a set). - Five atomic cards that you mostly get right will each appear less often over time, and each review is faster. A practical rule: if a card takes you more than ~10–15 seconds on average, it’s a candidate for splitting or rewording. Over time, you want most reviews in the 5–10 second range. If you want to control workload while splitting: - Temporarily reduce new cards/day. - Edit existing cards during reviews (“edit as you go”) instead of adding more new material. - Suspend the hardest 5–10% to clean up later.
Okay, but I’m still confused about “spaced repetition.” Like, I get the concept, but why is it so brutal? Why does it feel like I’m always behind?

Spaced repetition is “brutal” when the system is doing its job but your inputs are too heavy. Core idea: - Memory strength decays over time. - Reviewing right before you forget is efficient: you reinforce the memory with minimal time. - A scheduler estimates when you’re likely to forget and shows the card then. Why it feels endless: 1) You’re adding new cards faster than you can stabilize them. 2) Early in learning, the intervals are short, so new cards generate a spike of reviews. 3) If you fail a lot, cards get reset to shorter intervals, creating a feedback loop. 4) If you use cards that require exact phrasing or multiple facts, your failure rate goes up, and so does your workload. Spaced repetition isn’t “do everything every day.” It’s “do the right tiny subset today.” But you must control the inflow (new cards) and keep failure rate reasonable. A good target is: - Mature card success rate: ~85–95%. - New/learning cards will be lower, but if you’re failing tons, your card design or steps are off.
I think my success rate is bad because I’m constantly pressing Again. I feel guilty pressing Hard or Good when I didn’t get it perfectly.

That guilt is a major driver of overload. You need a grading policy that matches what you’re trying to achieve. Try this rubric: - Again: You could not recall the core answer at all, or you guessed, or you were wrong in a way that would mislead you later. - Hard: You got it basically right but it took too long, felt shaky, or you missed a minor detail. - Good: You recalled it correctly with reasonable effort. - Easy: Immediate, effortless recall. If your cards demand verbatim text, the system will force “Again” too often. So the real fix is: 1) Make answers flexible: ask for meaning, not the exact sentence. 2) Put “acceptable variants” on the back (synonyms, alternative wording). 3) Use short answers; avoid long paragraphs. You’re not being graded; you’re calibrating a scheduler. Over-penalizing yourself makes the scheduler think you’re forgetting constantly, so it shows you cards constantly.
But sometimes the difference between “core answer” and “minor detail” is fuzzy. I don’t trust myself to decide.

Then make the card decide for you by making the prompt and answer explicit. Techniques: 1) Add constraints on the card: - Front: “Give the ONE key reason for X.” - Back: “Reason: Y. (Extra detail: Z)” Now you’re only responsible for Y. 2) Use two cards: one for the core, one for the detail. - Card A: core concept. - Card B: the detail, with a strong cue. 3) Put a checklist on the back: - Back: “Must include: Y. Nice to include: Z.” If you got “must include,” you can safely press Good/Hard. 4) Use “prompt engineering” for yourself: - Instead of “Explain X,” ask “What is the definition of X in 1 sentence?” or “What are the 2 components of X?” Ambiguity is poison in Anki because it forces inconsistent grading and repeated lapses.
Okay. I think a lot of my cards are like “Explain [concept]” and then the back is a huge chunk of text. That’s probably bad.

That’s one of the worst common patterns. It becomes a reading exercise, not recall practice. Better conversions: - Turn “Explain X” into: a) “What is X?” (one-sentence definition) b) “What problem does X solve?” c) “What are the parts of X?” (2–4 bullets) d) “What’s an example of X?” e) “What’s a common confusion with X?” If you need the big chunk as reference, keep it—but don’t make it the “answer.” Put it under an “Extra” section, and keep the “Answer” section short and testable. A good template: - Front: question - Back: - Answer (1–3 lines) - Extra (optional longer explanation) - Source (optional link/page tag)
How do I actually restructure without spending forever editing? I already spend hours reviewing.

Use a triage workflow that edits only the cards that are actively hurting you. During reviews, when you hit Again or feel annoyed: 1) Stop and ask: “Why did I miss this?” - Too much info? - Vague prompt? - Need context? - Purely not learned yet? 2) If it’s a design issue, do a 30–90 second fix: - Split into two cards. - Rewrite the front to be specific. - Shorten the back to a crisp answer. - Add a hint or example. 3) If it’s just not learned: keep it, but consider adding a short learning step (like 10m, 1d) so you see it again soon without hammering Again repeatedly. 4) If it’s low value or repeatedly failing: suspend it and move on. Create a tag like “to_fix” and batch-fix later. This prevents “edit paralysis.” You don’t need to perfect the whole deck—just remove the sharp edges causing overload.
What about cloze deletions in general? Are they bad?

Clozes aren’t bad; they’re easy to misuse. Clozes are good for: - short factual statements with one missing piece, - equations/identities where structure matters, - vocabulary-like pairings, - “fill the missing term” when the context is stable. Clozes are risky for: - lists (all-or-nothing), - paragraphs (too many cues, shallow recall), - anything where multiple wordings are valid, - anything that should be generated from understanding rather than pattern completion. If you like cloze, use these rules: 1) One cloze per card most of the time. 2) Keep the surrounding context short but meaningful. 3) Avoid “cloze-overload” where you can guess from grammar. 4) Prefer “question cloze”: turn it into a question with a blank answer. Example transformation: - Bad: “X is defined as {{c1::long definition}}.” - Better: “Define X in one sentence: {{c1::short definition}}. (Extra: long definition)”
You mentioned settings earlier. If I change settings, will it mess up my existing schedule?

Some settings affect future scheduling decisions, but they won’t instantly reshuffle everything in a catastrophic way. The biggest immediate levers: - New cards/day: affects workload immediately. - Learning steps: affects how often new cards repeat before graduating. - Lapse steps: affects how punishing failures are. - Max interval: affects long-term spacing, not short-term overload. If you’re overwhelmed, your safest immediate changes: 1) New cards/day → 0–10 temporarily. 2) Add a learning step of 1 day if you currently only have minutes. This prevents “I saw it 3 times today and then not for a week.” 3) Adjust lapse handling so one failure doesn’t fully reset the card to baby steps repeatedly. Before changing anything, export your deck or at least note your current options. But yes, you can change settings without destroying progress.
I think my learning steps are like “1m 10m.” That’s it. I see a new card, I fail, it comes back, I pass, and then it disappears for a while and I forget again.

That explains a lot. “1m 10m” creates same-day familiarity but not next-day consolidation. A common improvement: - Learning steps: 10m, 1d (optionally 3d) - Graduating interval: 3d - Easy interval: 4–7d How it feels: - Day 0: you learn it, see it again in ~10 minutes. - Day 1: you see it again; if you pass, it’s much more likely to stick. - Then it moves to several days. This reduces the “I crammed it today and forgot tomorrow” cycle, which causes lapses and more reviews.
If I add 1d step, won’t that increase reviews because now I’m seeing cards tomorrow too?

It increases early reviews slightly but reduces failures later, which usually decreases total workload. You’re trading a cheap review (tomorrow) for avoiding expensive relearning (multiple lapses later). Think of it as preventive maintenance. If tomorrow’s review takes 6 seconds and prevents three future lapses that each cost 30–60 seconds plus frustration, it’s a win.
Okay. Another issue: I can’t get through my reviews some days. Then the next day it’s even worse and I panic.

That’s the backlog spiral. A few strategies: 1) Stop adding new cards immediately until you’re consistently finishing reviews. New cards are optional; due reviews are not, because they compound. 2) Use a “review cap” carefully. If you cap reviews too low, you create permanent backlog. Better to: - set new cards to 0, - and aim to clear due reviews daily, even if it takes time for a week. 3) If the backlog is massive: - Create a temporary “crisis mode” plan: - 7–10 days of 0 new cards. - Do reviews in multiple short sessions (e.g., 3×30 minutes). - Suspend leeches aggressively. - Reduce friction: shorter cards, fewer Again presses. 4) Use filtered decks for triage: - Make a filtered deck of overdue cards or leeches and handle them intentionally. Do you know how many “due” you have right now vs “learning”?
I have like 1200 due. Learning is like 80. It’s awful. I keep thinking I should just reset everything.

Don’t reset. Resetting feels clean but usually just recreates the same problem with added demoralization. With 1200 due, you need a backlog plan: - Set New cards/day = 0 for now. - Aim for a sustainable daily review target, like 200–300, depending on your speed. - Accept that it might take 4–7 days to dig out, possibly longer. Tactics to make 200–300 doable: 1) Speed up decisions: if you don’t know within ~10 seconds, hit Again (or suspend if it’s bad). 2) Edit only the worst offenders. 3) Use “Hard” more often instead of Again for near-misses, but only if the card is appropriate. 4) Use the “bury” feature so siblings don’t appear same day (important for cloze siblings). And yes, check if “Bury new siblings” and “Bury review siblings” are enabled. Cloze notes can create multiple cards that show up together and inflate workload.
I don’t think bury siblings is on. I sometimes get like three cloze cards from the same note in a row and it’s miserable.

Turn that on. It’s one of the highest quality-of-life settings for cloze-heavy decks. Why it matters: - Seeing sibling clozes back-to-back creates artificial ease (you remember from the previous card). - Then later, when they’re separated, you fail them. - It also makes sessions feel repetitive and longer. Enable: - Bury new siblings: ON - Bury review siblings: ON That will spread related cards across days, improving retention and reducing the feeling of déjà vu grind.
But if they’re spread out, won’t I forget the context and fail more?

If you rely on adjacent context to answer, that’s a sign the cards are too dependent on each other. Better to: - include just enough context on each card to stand alone, - but not so much that it becomes a paragraph. For clozes, add a short lead-in: - “In the context of [topic], … {{c1::answer}} …” Or put a hint field that you can reveal. The goal is: each card should be answerable without needing the previous card, but not so broad that it’s vague.
I think my clozes are made from the same paragraph so each one depends on the paragraph. How do I add context without making it huge?

Use “minimal sufficient context”: 1) Keep one sentence (or even a clause) that pins meaning. 2) Remove decorative words. 3) If the paragraph has multiple ideas, split into multiple notes. Practical editing method: - Copy the paragraph into a note. - Highlight the exact idea you want to remember. - Rewrite it as a single clean sentence in your own words. - Cloze one key term. Example (structure, not content): - Original: long paragraph with multiple claims. - Rewrite: “X causes Y by mechanism Z.” Then cloze Z, or cloze Y depending on what you want to retrieve. Also consider switching some clozes into Q→A cards. Q→A forces you to state the question clearly, which reduces ambiguity.
I avoided Q→A because it feels slower to make. Cloze is like copy/paste.

Cloze is faster to create, but slower to review if it’s messy. You’re paying later with interest. A compromise: - Keep cloze for simple “term ↔ definition” type facts. - Use Q→A for anything conceptual, multi-step, or where the question matters. If creation speed is important, use a consistent template: - Q: “What is X?” - A: “X is Y.” (1 sentence) - Extra: longer explanation After you do 20–30 like this, it becomes fast.
What about the “Again” interval? Sometimes I fail and it shows up in like 10 minutes, which is fine, but other times it feels like it keeps coming back forever.

That’s governed by lapse steps and relearn settings. A common issue is having multiple short lapse steps that keep the card in “relearning” too long, especially if the card is inherently hard or poorly designed. A reasonable lapse setup: - Lapse steps: 10m (and maybe 1d if you tend to forget overnight) - New interval: 70–80% (so you don’t drop all the way back) - Minimum interval: 1d - Leech threshold: 6–8 lapses, with “suspend leech” on This way, one failure doesn’t fully reset months of spacing, but you still get a quick reinforcement. Also: if a card fails repeatedly in one sitting, it’s often not a memory issue—it’s a card issue. Suspend and rewrite.
I have leeches but I ignore them because I feel like suspending is giving up.

Suspending is not giving up; it’s removing broken tools from your workflow. Leeches exist because repeated lapses mean one of: - the information isn’t well understood, - the card is ambiguous, - the card is too big, - the cue is weak, - or it’s not worth memorizing at that granularity. A good leech workflow: 1) When a card hits leech threshold, it auto-suspends. 2) Once a week, review suspended leeches: - Rewrite into 2–3 better cards, or - Decide it’s not worth it and delete. This keeps your daily reviews clean. Daily Anki should be boring and steady, not a constant fight.
I also sometimes add new cards right before bed and then I wake up with more reviews and I hate myself.

Timing matters less than consistency, but adding new cards at night can create a psychological “review hangover.” Try: - Separate “adding” from “reviewing.” - Add cards only after you finish reviews for the day. - Or designate specific days for adding and other days for catch-up. Also, “40 new/day because behind” is a mental trap. Anki is not a backlog-clearing tool; it’s a retention tool. The rate should match what you can maintain for months.
So how do I pick a sustainable new-card number?

Use a workload budget. Estimate: - Average time per review (seconds). - Daily time available. - Acceptable review count. Example method: 1) Track your average review time for 3 days (Anki stats show this). 2) Decide a daily time cap (e.g., 45 minutes). 3) Compute max reviews: (45 min * 60) / avg_seconds_per_review. If you average 8s/review: 270 reviews/day. Then pick new cards/day such that your total reviews stabilize under that cap. For many people: - 5–15 new/day is sustainable. - 20+ is sustainable only if cards are easy and you have time. Given you’re at 2–3 hours, you should go down to 0–10 new/day until your average review time drops and your due count stabilizes.
My average time per card is like 12 seconds. Sometimes more because I’m rereading long backs.

That’s another reason to shorten answers. If you can move from 12s to 8s, that’s a 33% productivity gain without “trying harder.” Quick ways to reduce time/card: - Put the answer at the top of the back. - Put extra explanation below a divider. - Use bolding for the exact thing you need. - Remove filler text. - Avoid multiple clozes. Also consider turning on “Show answer timer” to keep yourself honest. Not to rush, but to notice when a card consistently eats time.
I’m worried that if I make cards too short, I’ll lose understanding and just memorize fragments.

That’s a valid concern—Anki can create “fragment knowledge” if you only memorize isolated facts. The fix is not longer cards; it’s better structure: - Use short cards that connect to a mental model. - Add “why” or “contrast” cards occasionally. - Use examples. A balanced card set for a concept might include: 1) Definition (1 sentence) 2) Purpose/why it matters (1 sentence) 3) Key components (2–4 bullets) 4) Common confusion (X vs Y) 5) Minimal example (given scenario → apply concept) Each card is short, but together they build understanding. Also, Anki is not the only tool. Understanding often comes from reading/doing/practice; Anki is for keeping the key outputs accessible.
I think I use Anki as my main way to learn. Like I just make cards from everything and assume it’ll work.

That approach overloads almost everyone. Better pipeline: 1) Learn first (reading, watching, solving, writing summary). 2) Identify “retrieval-worthy” points: - definitions you must recall, - distinctions, - steps you must execute, - common pitfalls, - key numbers/thresholds (if applicable), - mapping of concepts (A causes B). 3) Make cards only for those. 4) Keep reference notes separately. If you turn everything into cards, you create a second full-time job.
How do I decide what’s retrieval-worthy without second-guessing everything?

Use criteria: - Frequency: will you need it often? - Foundational: does it unlock many other ideas? - Confusable: is it easy to mix up? - High-stakes: would forgetting it cause big problems? - Compressible: can it be tested in a clean, unambiguous way? If a detail fails these, keep it in notes, not Anki. Also impose quotas: - “Max 10 new cards per day.” Quotas force prioritization. You can always add later if it proves important.
I like the idea of quotas, but I’m anxious that I’ll miss something important.

The anxiety is understandable, but the alternative is unsustainable volume that makes you miss everything anyway. Two safety nets: 1) Tag by source/date so you can return later. 2) Keep a “parking lot” list of items you didn’t card. If they come up again, that’s evidence they’re important—then make a card. This turns “maybe important” into “proven important.”
Can we talk about Anki intervals? Sometimes I hit Good and it jumps to like 2 months, and I’m like, no way I’ll remember that.

Interval jumps are based on the card’s ease factor and your history. If a card has been correct multiple times, Anki assumes it’s stable. If you consistently feel the interval is too long, possible causes: - You’re pressing Good when you’re actually uncertain (should be Hard). - The card is too easy because it’s over-cued (e.g., too much context). - Ease factor is high due to many Easy presses earlier. - Your “interval modifier” might be high (less common if default). What to do: 1) Use Hard for shaky recall. 2) Reduce cues that allow guessing. 3) Avoid Easy unless it’s truly effortless. 4) Check “Starting ease” (often 2.5). If your deck has many too-easy cards, ease can stay inflated. But also: trust the algorithm a bit. Feeling uncertain about long intervals is normal. If you truly forget, you’ll relearn; that feedback will correct scheduling.
I think I used Easy a lot early because it felt good and I wanted to go faster.

That will inflate intervals and can cause later forgetting. You can recover: - Stop using Easy for a while. - If some cards are now “too jumpy,” you can use the “Set Due Date” or “Reschedule” tools, but I’d avoid mass changes. Let it normalize. More importantly, fix card difficulty. If a card is well-designed and you can recall it cleanly, long intervals are okay.
What about “FSRS”? I keep seeing people talk about it but it sounds complicated.

FSRS is a newer scheduling algorithm add-on/feature that can optimize intervals based on your personal forgetting curve. It can reduce workload for the same retention, but it’s not a magic fix for bad cards or too many new cards. If you’re already overwhelmed, priorities: 1) Reduce new cards. 2) Fix card design. 3) Clear backlog. Then consider FSRS. If you want the simple version: - FSRS uses your review history to fit parameters. - You pick a target retention (e.g., 0.9). - It schedules accordingly. It can be worth it once your workflow is stable, but don’t use it as a substitute for fundamentals.
So I should not mess with FSRS right now?

If your system is currently chaotic (1200 due, high lapses, messy cards), I’d stabilize first. FSRS will fit to noisy data and you won’t know what’s causing improvements. A good timeline: - Week 1: stop adding, clear backlog, enable bury siblings, adjust learning steps. - Week 2: redesign leeches and big cards, find sustainable new/day. - Week 3+: if you’re curious, try FSRS with a conservative retention target.
Okay. Another thing: I review on my phone a lot. I do it in bed, on the bus, whenever. Is that bad?

It’s fine, but it can contribute to mindless reviewing and sloppy grading. Two suggestions: 1) Do “new cards” only when you can focus (desk, not in bed). 2) Use phone reviews for mature cards or quick maintenance. Also, if you review when sleepy, you’ll press Again more, which inflates workload. Protect your Anki data from your worst mental states.
That’s true. Night reviews are brutal. But I don’t have time otherwise.

Then split sessions: - 10–15 minutes in the morning (fresh brain) for learning cards and hard reviews. - 10–20 minutes midday for routine reviews. - Optional small session at night for easy/mature cards only. Even small morning sessions can dramatically reduce night suffering because you’re catching learning cards at the right time.
I like schedules but I never follow them. I start strong and then fall off.

Then design for “minimum viable consistency.” Rules that are hard to break: - “I will do at least 20 reviews every day, no matter what.” - “I will not add new cards unless due is under X.” - “If I miss a day, next day is ‘reviews only’—no new.” The goal is to prevent backlog explosions. You don’t need perfect adherence; you need guardrails.
What should X be? Like what number of due reviews is acceptable before adding new?

Pick a threshold that keeps you calm and consistent. Common choices: - Add new only if due reviews < 100 or - Add new only after finishing all due reviews for the day Given your current situation, I’d do: - No new until due < 200 and stable for at least 5 days. Then reintroduce new cards slowly: 5/day for a week, then 10/day if stable.
I’m scared if I stop new cards I’m wasting time.

You’re not wasting time; you’re protecting the system that makes time useful. If you keep adding while drowning, you’re converting time into stress and forgetting. Also, clearing backlog improves learning speed because: - you stop seeing the same failed cards repeatedly, - your confidence improves, - and your average seconds/review drops.
Can you give me a step-by-step “fix my Anki” plan? Like literally what I should do tomorrow.

Tomorrow plan (60–90 minutes total, adjust as needed): 1) Change settings (10 minutes) - New cards/day: 0 - Enable bury new siblings + bury review siblings - Learning steps: 10m, 1d - Graduating interval: 3d - Easy interval: 5d (or similar) - Lapse steps: 10m (optional 1d) - Leech threshold: 8, action: suspend 2) First review session (25–40 minutes) - Do due reviews only. - If a card is long/vague and you fail or hesitate: edit quickly or tag “to_fix.” - If it’s a nightmare: suspend immediately. 3) Short break (5 minutes) 4) Second session (25–40 minutes) - Continue due reviews. - Aim for a fixed number (e.g., 200) rather than “until done,” so you don’t burn out. 5) End-of-day cleanup (5 minutes) - Check stats: how many Again presses? average time? Any patterns? - Write down 2–3 recurring card issues you noticed. Repeat for several days. Once due count is manageable, reintroduce 5 new/day.
If I suspend aggressively, won’t I lose coverage? Like I’ll have holes.

You’ll have fewer holes than you think because the cards you’re suspending are often the ones not working anyway. To feel safer: - Don’t delete; suspend. - Tag them “suspended_to_fix.” - Once per week, unsuspend a few, rewrite properly, then reintroduce. This turns “holes” into a manageable maintenance queue.
How do I rewrite a leech card properly? Like what’s the process?

Leech rewrite process: 1) Identify why it’s a leech: - unclear question? - too many facts? - no context? - confusing similar items? 2) Choose a card type: - If it’s a definition: Q→A. - If it’s a single term in a sentence: cloze. - If it’s a list: one-missing-item cloze or atomic cards. 3) Add a discriminative cue: - “In contrast to Y, X does what?” - “Given condition A, what happens?” - “What is the key difference between X and Y?” 4) Keep the answer short. 5) Add an example or hint in Extra. 6) Test the new card: can you answer it in <10 seconds? If not, simplify.
I think a lot of my leeches are because I confuse similar things. I have cards like “Difference between X and Y” and then the back is a table.

Tables are good reference but often too much for recall. Better: make multiple small comparison cards: - “One key difference: X has ___; Y has ___.” - “Which one is associated with ___? X or Y?” - “If you see feature ___, which one is it?” - “True/false: X implies ___.” Each card targets one discriminating feature. Keep the table in Extra for reference, but don’t demand full-table recall.
That makes sense. But I still worry I’ll end up with tons of tiny cards and no big picture.

Big picture comes from: - periodic synthesis (summaries, concept maps), - practice applying ideas, - and a small set of “overview” cards that are carefully designed. You can have a few “big picture” cards if you treat them as: - prompts for a short outline (not a full essay), - and you grade them gently (Hard vs Good) based on completeness. Example structure: - Front: “Outline the 3-part framework for ___.” - Back: “1) … 2) … 3) …” Keep it to 3–5 bullets, not 15. Then the details live in atomic cards.
I also do “image occlusion” sometimes. Those become nightmares because there are like 20 blanks.

Image occlusion is notorious for that. Fixes: 1) Don’t occlude everything. Only occlude the labels you truly need. 2) Limit siblings per image: - Make multiple images or multiple notes for different regions. 3) Use “hide one, guess one” rather than “hide all.” 4) Bury siblings is essential here. 5) If an image has 20 occlusions, consider deleting/suspending and remaking as 5–8 key occlusions. Also, add context text on the front: what you’re looking at and what you’re supposed to recall.
I think I made a bunch of occlusions because it felt like I was being thorough.

Thoroughness in card creation often translates into fragility in review. You want robust recall with minimal pain. A useful mindset shift: - Your goal isn’t to store everything. - Your goal is to reliably retrieve the most useful things with minimal daily cost.
How do I measure whether my changes are working?

Track a few metrics weekly: 1) Due reviews count trend (should go down then stabilize). 2) Average time per review (should decrease). 3) “Again” rate (should decrease). 4) Retention on mature cards (Anki stats show % correct; aim ~85–95%). 5) Subjective stress: are you dreading opening Anki? Also check “ease” distribution if you can. Many leeches and low ease indicate card issues.
What if my retention is high but I still have too many reviews?

Then you’re likely adding too many new cards or your target retention is too high. If retention is 95–98% and workload is too high, you can: - reduce new cards/day, - consider lowering retention target (if using FSRS), - or accept slightly more forgetting (e.g., press Good sooner, fewer Hard/Again for near-misses) if the cards are low-stakes. The “optimal” point is where the system is sustainable. A slightly lower retention with a sustainable habit beats perfect retention for two weeks followed by burnout.
I definitely burn out. I quit for a week and then come back to a mountain and then I quit again.

That pattern is exactly what sustainable settings and reduced inflow are meant to fix. Two anti-burnout tactics: 1) Make Anki smaller: - fewer new cards, - delete/suspend low-value material, - simplify cards. 2) Make Anki easier to start: - set a tiny daily minimum, - use a consistent time, - keep sessions short. If you can make it “too easy to skip,” you’ll stop skipping.
Okay but I still feel like I’m “behind” because I’m not adding enough.

“Behind” is a feeling created by an infinite syllabus mindset. Spaced repetition punishes that mindset because it requires long-term maintenance. Replace “behind” with “throughput”: - What can you reliably learn and retain per week? That’s your pace. Anything beyond that is fantasy and will collapse. If you want to increase pace later, you can: - improve card quality (faster reviews), - increase time budget, - or reduce other commitments. But first, stabilize.
I’m going to try 0 new cards for a while. But what do I do with stuff I want to remember in the meantime?

Keep a capture system that isn’t Anki: - A simple note titled “To Anki later.” - Bullet points only. - Once per week, convert the top priority items into 5–10 new cards. This keeps your brain calm (“I’m not losing it”) without feeding the daily review monster.
When I come back to making cards, how do I avoid repeating the same mistakes?

Use a pre-flight checklist for every card: 1) Is the question unambiguous? 2) Can the answer be said in 1–2 sentences or 2–4 bullets? 3) Is it testing one idea? 4) Is the cue strong enough that I know what I’m being asked? 5) Would I be okay grading it without guilt? (If not, it’s too vague.) 6) Could I answer it in <10–15 seconds? If it fails any, rewrite before adding.
I feel like I’ll spend forever on that checklist.

You won’t after a week; it becomes automatic. And you’ll save time in reviews. If you want it even simpler, use a single rule: - If I can’t decide between Again/Hard/Good confidently, the card is poorly designed.
That’s actually a good rule. Because half my time is me staring at the screen debating.
Exactly. Decision friction is a hidden time cost. Make cards that make grading obvious: - Either you recalled it or you didn’t. - Or you recalled it but slowly (Hard). No existential debates.
One more thing: sometimes I memorize the card formatting instead of the actual content. Like I recognize the shape of the cloze.

That’s “pattern recognition” rather than recall. Solutions: 1) Vary cues: make two cards from different angles (definition vs example). 2) Reduce over-cueing: don’t leave too many hints in the surrounding text. 3) Use “type the answer” for some cards to force active recall. 4) Shuffle context: avoid always using the same sentence structure. But don’t overdo it. Recognition isn’t always bad; it’s part of memory. You just want to ensure you can generate the answer without relying on superficial cues.
I’ve never used “type the answer.” Does it help?

It can, especially for short precise answers. It slows reviews a bit but increases retrieval strength and reduces “I kind of knew it” illusions. Use it selectively: - for items you keep misspelling/confusing, - for short phrases, - for things where precision matters. Don’t use it for long answers; that becomes tedious.
Okay. I’m going to change settings and stop new cards. But I’m still anxious about the 1200 due. How do I not freak out?

Treat it like a queue, not a verdict. A calming approach: - Pick a daily quota you can hit even on bad days (e.g., 150 reviews). - Every day you hit it, you’re winning. - Don’t look at the total too often; focus on the process. Also, you can “chip away” with micro-sessions: - 20 reviews when you open your phone. - 20 more at lunch. - 20 more later. Three micro-sessions is 60 reviews with low mental load. And remember: as you suspend/repair bad cards, your daily count will drop faster than you expect.
I’m worried suspending will reduce the due number artificially and I’ll feel like I’m cheating.

It’s not cheating; it’s quality control. The goal isn’t to make the due number zero. The goal is to maintain a set of useful, well-designed cards that you can keep up with. If a card is consistently wasting your time, removing it is rational.
I just checked and I have like 15,000 cards. That seems insane.

That’s huge for most people, especially with cloze siblings and image occlusion. It almost guarantees overload unless many are extremely easy or suspended. I’d do an audit: - How many are mature vs learning? - How many have lapsed multiple times? - Which tags/decks are the worst offenders? Then prune: - Suspend entire low-value categories. - Keep a core deck that you can maintain. You can always reintroduce later, but you can’t maintain everything indefinitely.
But how do I decide what to suspend at a category level without mentioning specifics? Like I don’t know what’s “core.”

Use behavior-based criteria rather than topic-based: - Suspend any deck/tag where: - average review time is high, - lapse rate is high, - cards are mostly long explanations, - you rarely feel payoff from remembering them. Also look at “ease” and “again” counts: - If a subset is generating disproportionate pain, it’s a candidate for pausing. You can also pick a “maintenance budget”: - Keep only enough cards that your daily reviews fit in your time cap. Everything else gets parked.
So basically I need to accept I can’t retain everything.

Yes. Sustainable spaced repetition requires choosing. The system is powerful, but not infinite. Your job is to build a small, high-quality memory set that supports your work. Not to mirror an entire textbook worth of details.
Okay. Can we talk about “overlearning”? Sometimes I keep hitting Again until it feels easy, like I want zero uncertainty.

Overlearning is when you keep practicing beyond what’s needed for long-term retention. It feels satisfying but costs a lot of time. Spaced repetition already handles reinforcement. Your goal during a review is not “make it feel easy now,” it’s “successfully retrieve and move on.” If a card is shaky: - Press Hard (if basically correct). - If wrong, press Again once, relearn, then move on. Don’t loop it multiple times in one sitting trying to achieve perfect comfort. If you want extra practice, do it outside Anki with targeted drills, then come back.
Sometimes I’ll see a card and it triggers a whole chain of thoughts and I start thinking about related stuff and then I lose time.

That’s common, especially with conceptual material. Two options: 1) If it’s useful elaboration, capture it quickly: - Add a brief note in the “Extra” field or a separate note. - But don’t let it derail the session. 2) If it’s distracting: - Use a timer (e.g., 25-minute Pomodoro). - Treat reviews as “answer, grade, next.” Reflection can happen after. Anki sessions are for retrieval practice, not deep study.
I feel like I’m doing everything wrong, honestly.

You’re doing the normal beginner-to-intermediate path: over-add, over-cloze, over-Again, then hit the wall. The fix is mechanical and very doable. If you implement just three changes, you’ll feel relief: 1) New cards/day down to near zero until stable. 2) Learning steps include 1 day. 3) Split or suspend the worst cards. Everything else is optimization.
Alright. I’ll do those tomorrow. If I come back and my due is still huge, what then?

Then you keep going with the backlog plan. The due number won’t vanish in a day, but it will start trending down if you stop adding and reduce lapses. If after a week it’s not improving, that indicates: - too many failures (card quality), - too much time spent per card (long answers), - or you’re not actually getting through enough reviews daily. Then we’d adjust: - more aggressive suspension, - more rewriting, - lower daily expectations but increase consistency, - or temporarily increase daily review time for a short sprint.
What if I just can’t do 200 reviews a day? Like mentally I can’t.

Then set a smaller quota that you can do every day, like 80–120. Consistency beats intensity. If the backlog persists, you can also: - use “Forget”/reset on a subset of very old overdue cards (carefully), - or suspend a chunk and reintroduce slowly. But I’d first try: - 100/day, every day, zero new cards, for two weeks. That alone often stabilizes things.
Okay. I think I can do 100/day.

Good. Make it frictionless: - Same time each day if possible. - Keep phone notifications off; just open Anki and do 100. - Stop exactly at 100 to avoid burnout. - Don’t add new cards. After a week, check: are reviews getting easier? Is the due number slowly decreasing? Is your average time dropping?
I’ll try. One last question: should I use multiple decks or just one big deck?

Multiple decks are fine, but they can encourage uneven attention and “deck hopping.” For scheduling, Anki handles per-deck options, so you can control new cards in specific areas. If you’re overwhelmed, one approach: - Keep one “core” deck with strict settings. - Put everything else in a “parking” deck with new cards set to 0. - Only promote cards from parking to core when you’ve proven they’re worth maintaining. This gives you control without losing material.
That’s smart. I have a bunch of subdecks and I always end up adding new cards in all of them.

Exactly—distributed inflow becomes invisible. A single intake point forces you to notice volume. Also, consider limiting total new cards across all decks rather than per deck. Otherwise you might be adding 10 in each and accidentally doing 50/day.
Yeah… that’s probably what happened.
Then you’ve already identified the root cause: uncontrolled inflow. Tomorrow: set all decks to 0 new, or at least ensure the parent deck controls the limit. Then focus on due reviews only.
Okay. I’ll do it. If I come back later, can I ask about making better cloze cards specifically?

Yes. When you’re ready, bring 2–3 examples (describe the structure, not the content if you prefer), and I’ll show you: - how to reduce to one cloze, - how to add just enough context, - how to avoid list traps, - and how to decide between cloze vs Q→A.
Thanks. I’m still overwhelmed but at least I have a plan.
Keep it simple: stop new cards, add a 1-day learning step, bury siblings, and do a small daily quota. You’ll feel the pressure drop once the failure loop breaks.
I did what you said for two days. New cards at 0. I did 120 reviews each day. Due went from 1200 to like 1050. But I still feel like it’s endless.

That’s actually strong progress: ~150 fewer due in two days while not adding new. It feels endless because the absolute number is still large, but the trend is what matters. At 120/day, 1050 due is roughly 9 days if nothing else changed. In reality, it may slow because new learning cards generate follow-ups, but you’ll also suspend/repair leeches, which speeds it up. To make it feel less endless: - Track a weekly delta: “Due decreased by X this week.” - Celebrate process metrics: lower Again rate, faster time, fewer rage cards. Also, are you still getting lots of repeated failures? Or is it mostly steady?
Still lots of failures. I feel like half the cards I see I press Again. And then they show up again and I fail again.

That suggests card design issues are dominating. If 50% Again, you’re essentially relearning everything daily. We need to reduce the failure rate. Immediate interventions: 1) Be more willing to suspend. If you fail the same card twice in one day, suspend it on the spot. That’s a broken card or not-yet-learned material. 2) Add a “hint” field or extra context for cards that are too bare. Sometimes the cue is too weak; the brain can’t find the memory. 3) Split “too much” cards. If you read the back and think “of course I couldn’t recall that,” it’s too big. 4) Adjust lapse steps so failures don’t create endless loops: - If your relearn step is too short, you’ll see it again before you’ve had time to reconstruct properly. 10m is okay, but if you’re failing again at 10m, it’s not time—it’s design/understanding. Can you describe what a typical failed card looks like? Is it a multi-cloze, a vague prompt, a giant back, or something else?

A lot are clozes with two blanks. Or a sentence where the blank could be like several different words and I’m supposed to guess the “right” one. Or it’s like “X leads to Y because Z” and I blank Y and Z.

Those are fixable patterns. 1) Two blanks in one sentence: Split into two cards, or make two separate sentences. The cognitive load doubles because you’re holding one blank while searching for the other. 2) Multiple valid answers: That’s an “ill-posed” card. Fix by: - narrowing the prompt (“specifically, what term is used for…”), - or listing acceptable answers on the back and allowing yourself to grade Good if you produce any acceptable variant. But the best is to rewrite so only one answer fits. 3) “X leads to Y because Z” with Y and Z blanked: Make two cards: - Card A: “X leads to what?” → Y - Card B: “Why does X lead to Y?” → Z Or make a cause→effect card and a mechanism card. Also consider turning causal chains into: - Front: “Given X, predict Y.” Back: “Y.” - Another: “What mechanism explains X→Y?” Back: “Z.”
But if I split everything, I’m rewriting my whole deck.

You don’t need to rewrite the whole deck—only the cards that are actively failing and consuming time. With your failure rate, that might still be a lot, but you can do it incrementally. A practical rule: - Each day, fix 5 cards. Just five. That’s manageable. Over two weeks, that’s 70 improved cards—often enough to noticeably reduce stress because the worst offenders are the ones you see repeatedly.
Okay, I can try fixing five. But I don’t know how to decide which five.

Choose the five that meet any of these: - You failed them today (Again). - You spent >20 seconds on them. - You felt annoyed/confused by the wording. - You’ve seen them multiple times recently. Anki can help: tag leeches, or sort by “lapses” in the browser and target the top offenders. Workflow: - During review, when you encounter a candidate, hit “Edit,” tag it “fix,” and move on. - Later, open the browser, filter “tag:fix,” and rewrite those five.
I didn’t know you could sort by lapses. That’s useful.

Yes—Browser → choose the column “Lapses” (you may need to enable columns). Then sort descending. Those are your pain generators. Also sort by “Interval” + “Lapses”: - A card with many lapses and a short interval is a chronic problem.
If I suspend a card, should I replace it immediately?

Not immediately. Suspension is to stop the bleeding. Replacement can happen in a weekly maintenance session. If you replace immediately during reviews, you risk: - spending too long editing, - adding more new cards, - and derailing your quota. So: - Suspend now. - Tag for later. - Weekly: rewrite and reintroduce 10–20 fixed cards.
I’m starting to see how this is like managing a system, not just “study harder.”
Exactly. Anki success is systems engineering: - input rate, - item quality, - feedback loops (lapses), - and maintenance. Once the system is stable, it feels almost boring—which is the ideal.
I want boring. I’m tired.

Then your next focus is reducing variability: - consistent daily quota, - no new cards for now, - fix a few pain cards daily, - suspend anything that loops. Within 1–2 weeks, you should feel a noticeable shift.
Okay. Another question: should I randomize reviews or do them in order?

Random is usually fine. But if you’re overwhelmed, you might benefit from prioritizing: - learning cards first (so they don’t pile up), - then due reviews. Anki already surfaces learning/relearning cards prominently. If you want more control, you can use filtered decks: - Filter: “is:due” or “prop:due<0” for overdue. - Another filter: “tag:leech” or “prop:lapses>5” But keep it simple: default order is okay unless you have a specific goal.
I think I’ll keep it simple. I’ll do 100 a day, fix five, and suspend repeat failures.
Good. After 7 days, check: - due count, - average time, - Again rate, - number suspended. If you report those numbers, I can help you adjust the plan.
Alright. I’ll come back in a week.
Sounds good. Keep the system small and kind to yourself—Anki should serve you, not the other way around.

It’s been a week. I did 100 reviews every day. Due went from 1050 to 620. Average time per card dropped from 12s to 9s. Again rate is still kind of high but better. I suspended like 90 cards. I fixed maybe 20.

Those are excellent system-level improvements: - Due down ~430 with steady 100/day is strong. - Time/card down 25% is huge. - Suspending 90 likely removed a lot of repeated pain. Now we can tune: 1) Increase fixes a little, but keep it bounded. If you can, fix 5 cards/day (35/week). But only if it doesn’t threaten consistency. 2) Lower Again rate further by addressing the main causes: - ambiguous prompts, - multi-fact cards, - insufficient context. 3) Consider cautiously reintroducing new cards only after due is <200 and your Again rate is reasonable. Not yet. Question: among the cards you still fail, what’s the dominant pattern now? Still multi-cloze? Or more “I never learned this”?
It’s more “I never learned this.” Like I made a card from something I read but I didn’t actually understand it, so when it comes up I’m blank.

That’s a key insight. Anki can’t substitute for initial learning. For those cards, you have three options: 1) Suspend until you properly learn the concept elsewhere. 2) Add a short “primer” to the card (in Extra) that makes it learnable. 3) Rewrite into simpler prerequisite cards. A good policy: - If you fail and realize you don’t understand it at all, suspend and tag “needs_learning.” Then schedule a separate learning session to build understanding, and only then reintroduce. Otherwise Anki becomes a daily reminder of confusion.
I don’t like suspending those because I feel like I’m avoiding the hard stuff.

Avoiding is only bad if you never come back. Suspending is fine if it creates a pipeline: - “needs_learning” queue, - weekly learning block, - then rewrite/reintroduce. Hard stuff requires a different mode than flashcards: explanation, examples, practice. Anki is for retrieval after that.
How do I do the weekly learning block without it becoming another overwhelming task?

Keep it small and ritualized: - 30 minutes once per week. - Pick 5 suspended “needs_learning” items. - Learn them properly (from your notes or a resource). - Create 5–10 clean cards max. Stop there. The goal is steady progress, not clearing the entire queue.
That sounds doable. I’m surprised how much better I feel with fewer reviews, even though I’m still not “done.”
That’s the real win: you turned it into a manageable habit. Once you get due under control and card quality improves, you’ll be able to choose your pace rather than be chased by the app.
When can I start adding new cards again? I miss the feeling of moving forward.

When two conditions hold for at least a week: 1) You finish your daily reviews consistently with low stress. 2) Due count is stable and not trending upward. A practical threshold: - Due < 200 - Again rate not extreme (say <20–25% overall; lower is better) Then add 5 new/day for a week. If reviews remain stable, increase to 8–10. If reviews spike, drop back.
Okay. I’m at 620 due so not yet. But I can see it.
Keep going. At 100/day, you’re roughly a week away from 200–300 due, assuming it continues to drop. Also: as you fix more cards, you’ll fail less, so the drop can accelerate.
I never thought I’d say this, but I’m kind of starting to like Anki again.
That’s the point where it becomes a tool instead of a burden. Keep protecting the system: controlled inflow, good card design, and regular maintenance.
I've been staring at a blank page for an hour. What are evidence-based strategies for getting past writer's block?

Treat it like a performance problem with predictable failure modes: unclear target, too-high cognitive load, perfectionism, low energy, poor cues, or avoidance. Evidence-backed strategies map to those. 1) Reduce cognitive load (externalize thinking) - Outline in fragments: write a “scratch outline” with bullets, not sentences. Aim for 20–40 bullets in 10 minutes. No hierarchy required. - Use placeholders aggressively: write “[example]”, “[stat]”, “[transition]”. This prevents stopping to solve everything at once. - Separate creation from evaluation: generate first, critique later. This is supported by research on divergent vs convergent thinking—mixing them reduces fluency. 2) Lower the activation energy (behavioral activation) - Set a tiny “starter” task: 5 minutes or 150 words, whichever comes first. The goal is inertia-breaking, not quality. - Use implementation intentions: “If it’s 9:00, then I open the file and write for 10 minutes.” Specific cues increase follow-through. 3) Control perfectionism (cognitive reappraisal) - Define “bad draft” success criteria: “I will produce a draft that answers the main points, even if messy.” - Use a pre-commitment: tell yourself you are not allowed to edit until you reach a word count or a time threshold. 4) Improve specificity (goal clarity) - Write a one-sentence “north star”: what a reader should be able to do/know after finishing. - Write 3–5 “sub-questions” the piece must answer. Then draft answers as standalone paragraphs. 5) Use timed constraints (Pomodoro variants) - 25/5 works for many, but for blocked states try 10/2 or 15/3 to reduce dread. - End each session by writing the next sentence you’ll start with. This reduces next-start friction. 6) Manage state (sleep, stress, attention) - 2 minutes of paced breathing (e.g., 4–6 breaths/min) can reduce physiological arousal that feeds avoidance. - Quick movement (stairs, brisk walk 5 minutes) increases alertness and can improve idea generation. If you tell me what tends to happen during the hour—are you thinking “I don’t know where to start,” “it’s not good enough,” “I can’t find the right phrasing,” or “I don’t feel like it”—I can tailor the intervention.
It’s mostly “I don’t know where to start,” and then I keep trying to craft the perfect opening sentence.

That’s a common pairing: ambiguity + perfectionism at the entry point. Two fixes: decouple the opening from the start, and create a “start scaffold.” A) Don’t start with the opening - Start with the easiest middle section: pick one sub-question and answer it bluntly in 5–8 bullets. - Write a “temporary opening” that’s intentionally wrong: “This draft starts here; real opening later.” It’s a psychological trick: you satisfy the brain’s need for a beginning without spending budget on it. B) Build a start scaffold in 7 minutes 1. North star (1 sentence): “After reading, someone can ___.” 2. Audience assumptions (3 bullets): what they already know, what they don’t, what they care about. 3. Promise (1 bullet): the main benefit or outcome. 4. Table of contents (5–8 bullets): headings as questions. Then write any section except the first. C) Anti-perfection constraints - “No deletions for 10 minutes.” You may only add. This prevents cycling on the first line. - Change the font or write in a separate scratch doc so it doesn’t feel “official.” Try this: in the next message, write your north star sentence and 6 question-headings. Keep them generic if you want. Then I’ll show you how to turn that into a draft plan.

North star: After reading, someone can reliably get unstuck and produce a usable draft. Questions: Why do people get blocked? What should you do first? How do you get momentum? How do you structure a long piece? How do you revise without spiraling? How do you build habits so it doesn’t happen again?

Good. That’s already a workable spine. Now convert each question into a “minimum viable answer” (MVA): 3–7 bullet points that are specific, testable, and actionable. The goal is to create raw material. Here’s a draftable plan with MVAs: 1) Why people get blocked? - Ambiguous target (no clear success definition). - Overloaded working memory (trying to solve structure + wording + research at once). - Threat response (perfectionism, fear of wasting time). - Low energy/attention (sleep, stress, distractions). - Task aversion (too big, unclear next step). 2) What should you do first? - Define success: one sentence + 3 constraints (length, tone, scope). - Choose a section to start (not the intro). - Do a 10-minute bullet dump under one heading. - Use placeholders; ban editing. 3) How do you get momentum? - Timebox (10–15 minutes). - “Only add” rule; no backspacing. - Write ugly transitions: “Next, we’ll cover…” - End with a starter sentence for next session. 4) How do you structure a long piece? - Use question-headings; each section answers one question. - Start with an outline that is “good enough.” - Use the “MECE-ish” check: do headings overlap? is anything missing? - Decide on throughline: problem → causes → solution steps → maintenance. 5) How do you revise without spiraling? - Separate passes: structure pass, clarity pass, style pass, proof. - Use a checklist; limit each pass to a time budget. - Track changes; keep a “cut pile” doc instead of deleting. - Stop rule: “When it meets the north star + constraints, publish/finish.” 6) How do you build habits? - Implementation intention schedule. - Trigger → routine → reward loop. - Maintain an idea bank. - Weekly review: what blocked you; adjust environment. Now, to get you writing immediately: pick section 2 (“What should you do first?”). Write it as a numbered list of 5 steps in plain language. No intro. No hedging. 8 minutes. Paste whatever you get, even if rough.

1. Decide what “done” means in one sentence. 2. Make a quick list of the sections you need. 3. Pick the easiest section and dump bullets. 4. Expand bullets into rough paragraphs without fixing wording. 5. Leave notes for what you need to fill in later.

That’s already usable. Next step is to add specificity so each step is executable under stress. Add: time limits, concrete outputs, and “if stuck, then…” branches. Here’s a tightened version (you can adapt): Step 1: Define “done” (2 minutes) - Output: 1-sentence north star + 3 constraints (length, tone, scope). - If you can’t define it, write: “A first draft that covers headings A–F in any order.” Step 2: Create a scratch outline (10 minutes) - Output: 6–10 question headings + 2–5 bullets under each. - Rule: bullets can be fragments; no full sentences required. Step 3: Start in the middle (15 minutes) - Output: 150–300 words under the easiest heading. - Rule: placeholders allowed; no editing. Step 4: Expand with “bridges” (15 minutes) - Output: add transitions and examples, even if clunky. - Trick: write “Because ___, therefore ___” to force logic. Step 5: Capture gaps (3 minutes) - Output: a “to-fill” list: missing facts, examples, definitions. - End by writing the first sentence you’ll type next time. Now: which part do you fail on most—Step 1 clarity, Step 2 outlining, Step 3 drafting, or Step 4 expanding? The intervention differs.
Step 3. Once I start, I keep rereading and tweaking. I can’t keep moving forward.

You’re slipping into evaluation mode. You need a hard separation plus friction against rereading. Techniques that work well: 1) Disable easy rereading - Write in a minimalist mode (full-screen, hide previous paragraphs if possible). - If you can’t hide text, scroll so you can’t see earlier sections; keep the cursor at the bottom. - Use a “no up-arrow” rule: hands stay away from navigation keys for a timed block. 2) Enforce “forward-only drafting” - For 10 minutes, you’re allowed to type only new words. If you want to change something, write a bracket note: “[fix wording]”, “[better example]”. - If you catch yourself rereading, write “KEEP GOING” and continue. Sounds silly, but it’s an interrupt. 3) Use a “drafting script” - Start each paragraph with one of these stems: - “The key idea is…” - “A common mistake is…” - “Do this by…” - “If you get stuck, try…” This reduces the need to craft perfect sentences. 4) Put editing into a later scheduled slot - Create a calendar appointment: “Edit pass 1 (structure) 30 min.” Your brain relaxes when it knows editing will happen, just not now. 5) Time pressure with a visible timer - 8-minute sprints with a countdown. The urgency reduces the temptation to polish. Try a micro-experiment: next sprint, write 200 words under any heading, but every sentence must be 12 words or fewer. Short sentences reduce perfectionism and keep you moving. Report back what happens.
I tried the short-sentence thing. It helped a bit, but I still kept going back to fix words. I feel almost itchy if I don’t.

That “itch” is a cue for a compulsion loop: discomfort → edit → relief. To break it, you need a substitute behavior that gives relief without derailing progress. Use a “scratchpad relief valve”: - Keep a separate line at the bottom called “Edit cravings.” - When you want to fix something, add a quick note there: “Change ‘reliably’ to ‘consistently’ in para 2.” Then return to drafting. This preserves the sense of control and reduces anxiety. Also implement a “delayed gratification” rule: - You may edit, but only after you earn it: e.g., after 250 new words, you get 2 minutes of editing. This uses reinforcement rather than suppression. If the itch is strong, add physical constraints: - Put your hands in “typing only” position; no mouse. - If you need to change a word, you must retype the whole sentence at the end rather than editing in place. That cost discourages micro-edits. Finally, reframe what the draft is: - A draft is not “writing”; it’s “material generation.” The output is raw clay. - Editing is “sculpting.” Different session, different mindset. Pick one: scratchpad relief valve or earned editing. Which feels more doable?
Earned editing sounds doable. How should I set the ratio so I don’t just edit forever once I start?

Set a strict budget and make the reward small enough that it doesn’t flip you into full editor mode. A workable starting protocol: - Draft block: 20 minutes (or 300–500 new words). - Edit reward: 3 minutes max, with a checklist. - Repeat for 2–3 cycles, then stop. Editing checklist (so you don’t wander): 1) Fix only obvious typos that impede reading. 2) Replace repeated words only if it’s glaring. 3) Add a missing transition sentence. Nothing structural, no reordering, no rewriting whole paragraphs. Use a timer for the edit reward and stop mid-sentence if needed. The point is to train that editing is bounded. If you find you still “fall in,” reduce the reward to 90 seconds and increase drafting to 25 minutes. Also, define “editing is allowed only on the last paragraph written,” not earlier sections. That keeps forward momentum. Want to run a 2-cycle plan now? If yes, tell me your available time (30, 45, 60 minutes) and we’ll script it.
I have 45 minutes.

Do this: Cycle 1 (20 min drafting) - Choose one heading. - Write continuously; placeholders allowed. - Goal: 350 new words. Edit reward 1 (3 min) - Only last paragraph. Use checklist. Timer hard stop. Cycle 2 (20 min drafting) - Continue same section or start a new one if you’re stuck. - Goal: another 350 words OR finish the section with a rough conclusion sentence. Edit reward 2 (2 min) - Again, only last paragraph. Wrap (0–2 min) - Write the first sentence you’ll start with next time. - Write 3 bullet “next steps” (what to add, where). To increase compliance: before starting, write at the top: “Drafting mode. Editing at T+20 only.” This is a commitment cue. After you do it, tell me: did the itch decrease, stay the same, or increase? And did the writing quality feel worse, same, or better than expected?
I did two cycles. The itch decreased a bit in the second cycle. Quality felt worse while drafting but when I reread it wasn’t as bad as I feared.

That’s exactly the pattern you want: your subjective quality meter is unreliable mid-draft because it’s influenced by anxiety and comparison to an imagined polished version. Next, stabilize this into a repeatable process and address “quality panic.” 1) Add a “quality reassurance” metric After each drafting block, score only: - Did I produce new material? (yes/no) - Does it answer the section question? (0/1) - Are there placeholders for missing stuff? (0/1) If you hit 2/2, the block is a success regardless of style. 2) Normalize the “draft feels bad” sensation Make it explicit: “Drafting feels messy by design.” This reduces the surprise and the urge to fix. 3) Build a revision pipeline so you trust the process Create 3 revision passes with different goals: - Pass A: Structure (headings, order, missing sections). - Pass B: Clarity (topic sentences, logic, examples). - Pass C: Style (word choice, rhythm) + proof. Because you know style gets its own pass, you can tolerate ugliness earlier. If you want, paste one rough paragraph (no context needed) and I’ll show how to revise it in Pass B vs Pass C so the distinction becomes concrete.
I’d rather not paste text. Can you give me a generic example of how the passes differ?

Sure—here’s a generic demonstration. Raw draft paragraph (messy): “People get stuck because they try to do everything at once and then it feels like it has to be perfect, and also they don’t know what they’re trying to say. So they open the document and just stare or they tweak the first sentence. The fix is to lower the bar and make a plan but also to write anyway even if it’s bad.” Pass A: Structure (does the paragraph belong here? what’s its job?) - Decide purpose: This paragraph should explain causes of getting stuck. - Split into subpoints: (1) cognitive overload, (2) perfectionism, (3) unclear goal. - Maybe it becomes three bullets or three short paragraphs, each with its own heading. Rewritten for structure: - Cause 1: Cognitive overload—trying to plan, research, and phrase simultaneously. - Cause 2: Perfectionism—treating the first sentence as a final product. - Cause 3: Goal ambiguity—not knowing what “done” looks like. Pass B: Clarity (logic and comprehension) Take one cause and make it explicit with a clear claim + explanation + consequence. Example (clarity rewrite): “A common cause of getting stuck is cognitive overload: you try to decide the structure, find the right wording, and verify details at the same time. That overload makes every sentence feel high-stakes, so you default to the easiest activity—tweaking what’s already on the page—rather than adding new material.” Pass C: Style (word choice, rhythm, concision) Now polish: “Writer’s block often starts as cognitive overload. When you try to structure, phrase, and fact-check simultaneously, every sentence feels expensive. So you do the only safe thing: polish the first line instead of moving forward.” Notice: Pass A changes what exists; Pass B changes meaning clarity; Pass C changes how it sounds. To make this operational, you can label your sessions: “Today is Pass A only.” That prevents accidental line-editing.
I like the idea of labeling sessions. But sometimes I only have 20 minutes. What should I do in short sessions?

Short sessions should prioritize reducing future friction. Pick tasks with high leverage per minute. Three good 20-minute options: Option 1: Micro-draft (best if you can write) - 15 minutes forward-only drafting (200–300 words). - 3 minutes capture gaps (placeholders list). - 2 minutes write next-start sentence. Option 2: Outline tightening (best if starting feels hard) - 10 minutes: convert headings into questions. - 8 minutes: add 2 bullets under each question. - 2 minutes: choose the next section to draft and write its first sentence. Option 3: Structure pass (best if you already have text) - 15 minutes: reorder headings, merge overlaps, mark missing sections with “TODO: section.” - 5 minutes: write a “bridge paragraph” bullet list between two sections. Rule of thumb: - If you have zero words: outline. - If you have some words but no flow: structure. - If you have flow: draft. Also, begin with a 30-second “re-entry ritual”: - Read only the last 3–5 sentences you wrote, not the whole thing. - Then write the next sentence. This prevents spiraling into rereads.
My biggest issue is re-entry. If I read too much, I start judging. If I read too little, I forget what I was doing.

Use a re-entry artifact so you don’t need to reread the whole document. At the end of each session, leave: 1) A “continuation line”: the exact next sentence starter, like “Next, I’ll explain…” 2) A “state note” (1–2 bullets): what you were trying to do and what’s missing. 3) A “next 3 moves” list: - Add example for X - Define term Y - Write transition to section Z Then re-entry becomes mechanical: - Read the state note (not the prose). - Read the continuation line. - Start typing. If you don’t have these yet, create them retroactively: - Spend 5 minutes writing a state note for where you are now. Additional trick: create a “map at the top” At the top of the document, keep a 5-line map: - North star - Current section you’re drafting - Sections done - Sections pending - Biggest open question This reduces memory load and the need to reread.
How do I keep the map from turning into another procrastination tool?

Constrain it with a fixed format and a time cap. Rules: - Max 5 lines, max 12 words per line. - Update only at the end of a session, not the beginning. - Time limit: 60 seconds to update; if you can’t, leave it. Also, make it “binary” rather than descriptive: - Use checkboxes: [ ] drafted [ ] revised [ ] final - Use short labels: “Pending: transitions, example, conclusion.” If you notice yourself polishing the map, that’s a signal you should draft instead. Put a note: “Map is good enough; draft now.”
I also struggle with long-form structure. I either over-outline and never draft, or I under-outline and get lost.

Use a two-stage outline: “skeleton” then “muscle.” It prevents both extremes. Stage 1: Skeleton outline (10–15 minutes) - 6–10 headings as questions. - For each heading, write a one-sentence answer (even if crude). That’s it. No sub-bullets. Stage 2: Muscle outline (15–25 minutes, optional) - For only the next section you’ll draft, add: - 3 bullets: key points - 1 bullet: example/analogy - 1 bullet: common mistake This keeps detail local, not global. Drafting rule: - You may only expand the “next” section. Do not build muscle for later sections until you’ve drafted the current one. That prevents outline-as-avoidance. If you under-outline and get lost mid-paragraph, insert a “signpost” sentence: - “This section has three parts: A, B, C.” Then write A, B, C as subheaders or numbered items.
When I try to write signposts, they feel awkward and robotic.

They’re allowed to be awkward in drafts. But you can make them feel natural with a few patterns: - Preview with a benefit: “To make this practical, focus on three moves…” - Use a question: “So what do you do when you’re stuck? Start with three steps.” - Use a constraint: “In the next 10 minutes, you only need to do three things…” - Use parallel phrasing: “First… Second… Third…” Also, you can hide signposts as headings rather than sentences. Headings do the structural work without sounding robotic. During revision, you can soften them: Draft: “This section has three parts: A, B, C.” Revised: “Think of this as three quick moves.”
I’m pretty sure my block is also about attention. I get distracted and then it’s hard to resume.

Then treat it as attention design, not willpower. Environment controls (high impact): - Single-task setup: only the writing app + timer. Everything else closed. - Phone out of room or in focus mode. If it’s within reach, it will tax attention. - Use a “distraction capture” note: when you think of something else, jot it down and return. This reduces fear of forgetting. Session design: - Shorter sprints (10–15 minutes) with explicit breaks. - During breaks, do something that resets attention (stand, water) rather than something that hijacks it (scrolling). Cognitive trick: - The “2-minute rule”: if you feel an urge to switch tasks, wait 2 minutes while continuing to type anything relevant (even “I’m stuck because…”). Urges often decay. Also consider time-of-day: many people have a predictable 90–120 minute window of best focus. If possible, schedule drafting there and do outlining/revision in lower-energy times.
I can’t always choose the time. Sometimes I’m just depleted.

Then you need “low-energy modes” that still move the work forward without requiring peak creativity. Low-energy task menu (choose one): - Convert bullets into sentences (mechanical expansion). - Write transitions between existing sections. - Create the “to-fill” list and assign each item a source or action. - Do a structure pass: reorder headings, merge duplicates. - Write examples based on generic templates: “For example, imagine…” Also, reduce the bar explicitly: - “Today’s goal: produce a readable but boring draft.” Boring is fine; it can be improved later. And use energy resets: - 5 minutes light movement + water can sometimes shift you from depleted to functional. - If truly exhausted, do 10 minutes only and stop. Consistency beats hero sessions.
How do I measure progress without obsessing over word count?

Use process metrics and artifact metrics. Process metrics (behavioral): - Number of drafting sprints completed. - Minutes in drafting mode vs editing mode. - Number of re-entries without getting stuck. Artifact metrics (output quality proxies): - Sections drafted (checkboxes). - Questions answered (your headings are questions—mark them as answered/partial/unanswered). - Placeholder list shrinking over time. A simple dashboard: - Today: 2 sprints, 1 section drafted, 5 placeholders captured. - This week: 8 sprints, 3 sections drafted, 2 sections revised. Word count can be one metric, but it’s noisy. If you track “sections moved from outline → draft → revised,” you’ll see real progress.
I’m also unsure how to handle research or facts. If I stop to look things up, I derail.

Separate “drafting” from “fact-finding.” During drafting: - Insert a placeholder tag: “[verify: claim about X]” or “[need stat]”. - Keep a running “research queue” list, numbered. During a research session (separate time block): - Pull up the queue and resolve items one by one. - For each resolved item, paste the fact + source note into a “fact bank” section. - Only after you have 3–5 facts, do a quick integration pass. This prevents context switching. The brain hates switching between generative writing and analytical searching. If you must look something up mid-draft, set a 3-minute timer and allow only one query. If not found, leave it as a placeholder and move on.
What about when I feel like I don’t have anything original to say?

Originality is often recombination + clarity, not novelty. Long-form value comes from synthesis and usefulness. Frameworks to generate “your angle” without forcing genius: - “Friction log”: list 5 specific points where people get stuck; address them. - “Mistakes and fixes”: common failure modes + remedies. - “Decision tree”: if X is the cause, do Y. - “Checklist”: turn messy advice into a sequence with constraints. - “Before/after”: show a rough vs improved version (even generic). Also, define a narrow promise: “This will help you do X in Y minutes.” Specificity creates value. If you’re worried about repeating common advice, your differentiation can be: - Better structure. - More concrete steps. - Clearer constraints and examples. - A repeatable workflow.
I tend to write too abstractly. How do I force myself to be concrete?

Use “concreteness triggers” at the paragraph level. For each section, require: - One number (time, count, ratio, threshold). - One example (even hypothetical). - One observable behavior (“close tabs,” “write 10 bullets,” etc.). - One failure mode (“if you start editing, do…”). Templates: - “Do X for Y minutes, aiming for Z output.” - “If you notice A, then do B.” - “Here’s what it looks like: …” - “Common mistake: … Fix: …” You can also run a “concreteness pass” after drafting: - Highlight vague verbs: “improve,” “focus,” “refine,” “think.” - Replace with actions: “write,” “delete,” “reorder,” “timebox,” “label.”
Sometimes I feel resistance to even opening the document.

That’s pre-task avoidance. Solve it with a minimal start ritual and reduced stakes. Start ritual (2 minutes total): 1) Open the document. 2) Set a 10-minute timer. 3) Write a “warm start” paragraph: “What I’m trying to do in this section is…” 4) Copy the continuation line if you have one. Make the goal: “show up and write for 10 minutes.” Not “make progress.” Also reduce stakes: - Write in a scratch doc first. - Rename the file “Draft v0” so it doesn’t feel final. - Promise yourself you can stop after 10 minutes with no guilt. Often the resistance is to the imagined marathon. When the task becomes “10 minutes,” it’s tolerable.
I’m worried that if I allow myself to stop after 10 minutes, I’ll always stop.

Use a two-tier commitment: minimum and optional extension. - Minimum: 10 minutes (non-negotiable). - Bonus: extend in 10-minute increments if you feel okay.
